title: The PHP request lifecycle and statelessness
description: Classic PHP boots, handles one request, and tears down, so in-process state does not persist between requests.
body: Use when reasoning about caching, singletons, or state in PHP. The traditional model is shared-nothing: PHP-FPM/mod_php boots the framework, serves a single request, and discards all in-memory state, so a static property or in-process cache does not survive to the next request and must not be relied on for cross-request state. Persistent state lives in a database, cache server (Redis), or session store. Long-running runtimes (Swoole, RoadRunner, Octane) break this assumption by keeping the app in memory, which then exposes static/singleton state leakage between requests. Do not store per-request data in static properties. Actions: keep cross-request state in external stores and audit statics when moving to a persistent runtime. Validation signals include a test that per-request state does not leak. Risk boundary: leaked static state under Octane/Swoole can mix one user's data into another's response.
